title: Wet-felted merino bowl
subtitle: A small bowl formed over a resist, finished without seams
excerpt: Wrap merino roving around a polystyrene-ball resist, wet-felt the outside, then cut the opening and remove the resist. The ball resist method produces a seamless bowl with a firm, rounded base that holds its shape without a frame.
sourceNotes: The resist method for 3D wet-felted forms is the standard approach taught across introductory felting workshops. Polystyrene ball resists became common in domestic felting after the 1990s; the technique draws from the broader tradition of Mongolian and Anatolian felt-over-resist work. No single PD text documents this exact method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A form placed inside or under wet-felted roving to shape the finished piece. The roving is felted around the resist, then the resist is removed to reveal the hollow interior or shaped surface.
A technique that uses hot water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibre scales to open, migrate, and permanently lock together into a non-woven fabric.

content.text: This bowl is worked around a polystyrene-ball
. The roving is layered over the ball in cross-hatched sections,
until firm, and then the top is cut open and the ball is removed. The result is a seamless bowl about 10 cm across and 6 cm deep, suitable for a jewellery dish, a key bowl, or a decorative piece.

attrs.title: Finished size
attrs.body: A 9 cm polystyrene ball resist produces a finished bowl approximately 10-12 cm across at the rim and 5-7 cm deep, after approximately 20-25% shrinkage during felting. The roving layout extends about 5 cm beyond the equator of the ball on all sides before felting.

items.name: Merino roving
items.qty: 50-60 g (two or three colours if you like)

items.name: Tea towel or absorbent cotton cloth

content.text: Step 1: Lay roving over the ball

Lay the polystyrene ball on the bamboo mat. Pull off lengths of roving about 30 cm long and drape them over the top of the ball like the spokes of a wheel, so each strip runs from one side of the ball down over the top and down the other side. Lay 8-10 spokes in one direction, covering the ball evenly. Then lay a second set of spokes at 90 degrees to the first, creating a cross-hatched layer over the ball. Add a third layer at 45 degrees for the densest coverage. The roving should extend at least 5 cm past the equator of the ball on all sides.

Cover the laid roving with the felting net if you have one. Rub the soap bar over the net to leave a soapy layer, then pour hot water over the layout until the roving is thoroughly saturated. Press the roving gently against the ball with flat hands to flatten any standing tufts.
Step 3: Rub the surface for 15-20 minutes

Remove the net after 5 minutes of rubbing and work directly on the felted surface. Rub all sides of the ball with firm, even strokes: the top, the sides, and the underside (hold the ball in one hand and rub the other). Turn the ball frequently to work every surface. After 10-15 minutes, the roving should pass the pinch test: lift a small amount of surface fibre between finger and thumb. If no individual fibres pull free, the felt has set. If fibres pull away, continue rubbing.
Step 4: Cut the opening and remove the resist

Once the felt has set around the ball, mark a circle at the top using a felt-tip pen on the felt surface, or simply estimate a circle about 6 cm across at the top of the ball. Cut around this circle with sharp scissors, cutting through the felt only (not into the polystyrene). Push the polystyrene ball out through the opening. The bowl should hold its shape. If the rim tears as you remove the ball, the felt has not set sufficiently: replace the ball, continue felting for 5 more minutes, and try again.
Step 5: Full the bowl and shape the rim

With the resist removed, continue rubbing the inside and outside of the bowl with soapy hot water to full it further and tighten the structure. Roll the bowl inside the bamboo mat for 2-3 minutes, then re-shape by hand while wet: press the base flat on a hard surface, pinch the rim into a circle, and press the sides evenly. Rinse under cool running water, squeeze out, and dry upside down over a small bowl or jar that matches the interior diameter to hold the shape while drying.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Three problems specific to the ball-resist method.

items.symptom: Roving slips off the ball during wetting
items.cause: Too much water added too quickly before the fibres have any cohesion. The soap lubricates the fibres but without initial friction they slide.
items.fix: Wet the roving in stages: a small amount of water to dampen first, then rub briefly with soap before adding more. The friction from the soap film holds the fibres in place better than water alone.

items.symptom: Bowl tears when you cut the opening
items.cause: The felt has not fully set. The fibres at the cut edge are still loose and pull apart under scissors pressure.
items.fix: Re-wet the bowl with hot water, soap well, and continue rubbing for another 5-10 minutes before cutting. The felt needs to resist tearing at the cut edge before the opening is safe to cut.

items.symptom: Bowl is flatter than expected after drying
items.cause: The bowl was dried flat rather than over a former.
items.fix: Re-wet the dry bowl under warm running water, reshape while wet, and dry again over a jar or upturned bowl of the right diameter.
